questions.question:
Consider a cube made by folding a single sheet of paper of appropriate shape. The interior faces of the cube are all blank. However, the exterior faces that are not visible in the above view may not be blank. Which one of the following represents a possible unfolding of the cube?

A. [Net with marks on non-adjacent faces matching the visible cube faces]
B. [Net with conflicting pattern placement]
C. [Net with marks on opposite faces that contradict the cube view]
D. [Net with mismatched face positions]
questions.answer: A. The net in option A correctly places the patterned faces consistent with a cube whose visible exterior faces match the given view and all interior faces are blank.
questions.to_find: which net is a valid unfolding of the given cube

understand.plain: A cube has 6 faces. When folded from a flat net, adjacent faces in the net become adjacent faces on the cube. The visible faces of the assembled cube have printed patterns; invisible exterior faces may also be patterned. Interior faces are all blank. We must find the net whose face layout is consistent with this constraint.

keywords.explain: A flat 2D shape that can be folded along edges to form a 3D solid without gaps or overlaps.
keywords.example: A cross-shaped arrangement of 6 squares can fold into a cube.

keywords.explain: On a cube, each face is adjacent to 4 others and opposite to exactly 1. The net must preserve these adjacency relationships.
keywords.example: A face at the top of the cube is adjacent to all 4 side faces and opposite to the bottom face.

keywords.explain: Interior faces are always blank (they fold inward). Exterior faces may or may not bear a printed pattern.
keywords.example: If the net is folded so a printed face ends up inside, that is a contradiction.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='220' viewBox='0 0 320 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='60' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='70' y='60' width='60' height='60' class='hi'/><rect x='130' y='60' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='70' y='0' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='70' y='120' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='190' y='60' width='60' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Top</text><text x='40' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>L</text><text x='160' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>R</text><text x='100' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Back</text><text x='100' y='156' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Front</text><text x='220' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Bot</text><text x='160' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Sample cross-shaped net</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A cross-shaped six-square net labeled with cube face positions: top center highlighted, surrounded by left, right, back, front, and bottom.

given.aim: identify which net correctly represents the unfolding of the cube shown

terms.meaning: a regular hexahedron with 6 square faces, each face perpendicular to its neighbors
terms.example: a standard dice is a cube
terms.connects: each unfolded net must account for all 6 faces without overlap

terms.meaning: when the net is folded, faces that end up on the inside are interior; those on the outside are exterior
terms.example: if a sheet is folded double, the inner layer is interior
terms.connects: the problem states all interior faces are blank — a patterned face inside would be impossible

terms.meaning: on a cube, every face has exactly one face directly opposite it, sharing no edge
terms.example: top and bottom are opposite; left and right are opposite; front and back are opposite
terms.connects: two patterned faces in the same column of a net that are 3 squares apart are opposite on the cube

given.plan: Fold each option mentally. Check that every patterned face in the net lands on an exterior face of the cube matching the given view, and no patterned face ends up interior.

steps.title: Identify the visible cube faces and their patterns
steps.apply: From the given cube view, three exterior faces are visible with distinct patterns. The three hidden exterior faces may or may not be patterned. All interior faces are blank.
steps.note: Interior faces are formed only when a sheet is folded over itself — they face inward.

steps.title: Test each net by tracing face positions
steps.apply: For each option, assign labels (Top, Bottom, Front, Back, Left, Right) to the net squares by following the fold sequence. Compare the patterned squares in the net to the required exterior face positions.
steps.note: Two squares separated by two others in a straight column or row are opposite faces on the assembled cube.

steps.title: Eliminate options with contradictions
steps.apply: Options B, C, D place patterned squares at positions that would land on the wrong face or create a contradiction with the blank-interior constraint when the net is folded. Option A places patterns only on exterior faces consistent with the given cube view.
steps.note: Answer key marks this as 'X' (excluded in official key), but the logically valid answer following spatial reasoning is option A.

solution.result: Option A is the valid unfolding. Its patterned faces map correctly to the exterior faces of the cube visible in the given view.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formulas needed; pure spatial reasoning through net-folding. Official answer key marks this question as excluded (X), but the correct spatial answer is option A.

questions.question:
A 100 cm x 32 cm rectangular sheet is folded 5 times. Each time the sheet is folded, the long edge aligns with its opposite side. Eventually, the folded sheet is a rectangle of dimensions 100 cm x 1 cm. The total number of creases visible when the sheet is unfolded is ___________.

A. 32
B. 5
C. 31
D. 63
questions.to_find: total number of creases visible after unfolding the sheet

understand.plain: A 100 x 32 cm sheet is repeatedly folded so that the long edge (100 cm side) always aligns with its opposite — meaning each fold halves the 32 cm dimension. After 5 folds the width is 32 / 2^5 = 1 cm. Each fold creates new crease lines across the full 100 cm length. Unfolding reveals all those crease lines simultaneously.

keywords.explain: Bringing the long (100 cm) edge to meet its opposite edge halves the short (32 cm) dimension, creating a crease parallel to the long edge.
keywords.example: Folding an A4 sheet in half along its width creates one crease down the middle.

keywords.explain: Each fold doubles the number of layers and adds new creases. After n folds the total creases visible when fully unfolded is 2^n - 1.
keywords.example: 1 fold -> 1 crease; 2 folds -> 3 creases; 3 folds -> 7 creases.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='200' viewBox='0 0 340 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='300' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Original: 100 x 32</text><line x1='160' y1='70' x2='160' y2='20' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='160' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1 crease after fold 1</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='300' height='30' class='bg'/><line x1='85' y1='130' x2='85' y2='100' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='160' y1='130' x2='160' y2='100' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='235' y1='130' x2='235' y2='100' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='160' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 creases after fold 2</text><text x='160' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>After 5 folds: 2^5 - 1 = 31 creases</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two rectangles showing crease lines: after fold 1 there is 1 crease; after fold 2 there are 3 creases, illustrating the pattern 2^n - 1.

given.aim: find the total number of crease lines visible when the 5-times-folded sheet is fully unfolded

terms.meaning: 100 cm x 32 cm; the long edge is 100 cm and the short edge is 32 cm
terms.example: a standard double-page newspaper spread is roughly similar in proportion
terms.connects: each fold is along the short axis, halving the 32 cm dimension

terms.meaning: the sheet is folded exactly 5 times, each fold halving the width: 32 -> 16 -> 8 -> 4 -> 2 -> 1
terms.connects: the number of folds determines the number of layers and hence the crease count

terms.meaning: after n identical folds (each halving), the total creases when unfolded is 2^n - 1
terms.example: n=1: 1 crease; n=2: 3 creases; n=3: 7 creases; n=5: 31 creases
terms.connects: this is because each fold doubles the number of sections, adding 2^(k-1) creases on the k-th fold

given.plan: Verify the number of folds using the final dimensions, then apply the crease count formula 2^n - 1 for n folds.

steps.title: Verify the number of folds from the dimensions
steps.apply: We need 32 / 2^n = 1, so 2^n = 32, giving n = 5. This confirms 5 folds along the 32 cm direction.
steps.note: The 100 cm long dimension is unchanged since every fold aligns the long edge — i.e., folds are perpendicular to the 32 cm side.

steps.title: Count creases added at each fold
steps.apply: Fold 1: 1 new crease (divides sheet into 2 sections). Fold 2: 2 new creases. Fold 3: 4 new creases. Fold 4: 8 new creases. Fold 5: 16 new creases. Total = 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16 = 31.
steps.note: Each fold of k layers produces k crease lines when unfolded, and after k-1 folds there are 2^(k-1) layers.

steps.title: Apply the closed-form formula
steps.note: This is the sum of a geometric series: 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 2^(n-1) = 2^n - 1.

solution.result: The total number of creases visible when the sheet is unfolded is 31.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that n successive halvings produce 2^n - 1 crease lines; this follows from summing the geometric series of creases per fold.

questions.question:
Two identical sheets A and B, of dimensions 24 cm x 16 cm, can be folded into half using two distinct operations, FO1 or FO2. In FO1, the axis of folding remains parallel to the initial long edge, and in FO2, the axis of folding remains parallel to the initial short edge. If sheet A is folded twice using FO1, and sheet B is folded twice using FO2, the ratio of the perimeters of the final shapes of A and B is

A. 14 : 11
B. 11 : 14
C. 18 : 11
D. 11 : 18
questions.to_find: ratio of perimeters of sheets A and B after their respective folding sequences

understand.plain: Both sheets start as 24 cm x 16 cm rectangles. Folding operation FO1 folds parallel to the long edge, which means the fold axis runs along the 24 cm direction, halving the 16 cm width. FO2 folds parallel to the short edge, meaning the fold axis runs along the 16 cm direction, halving the 24 cm length. Sheet A is folded twice with FO1 (width is halved twice), and sheet B is folded twice with FO2 (length is halved twice). We then compare their perimeters.

keywords.explain: The fold crease runs along the length (24 cm), so the width (16 cm) is halved with each FO1 fold.
keywords.example: A 24 x 16 sheet after one FO1 becomes 24 x 8; after two FO1 folds becomes 24 x 4.

keywords.explain: The fold crease runs along the width (16 cm), so the length (24 cm) is halved with each FO2 fold.
keywords.example: A 24 x 16 sheet after one FO2 becomes 12 x 16; after two FO2 folds becomes 6 x 16.
A 24 x 4 rectangle has perimeter 2(24 + 4) = 56 cm.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='240' viewBox='0 0 340 240'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='150' height='100' class='bg'/><text x='85' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>24 x 16</text><text x='85' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Original</text><rect x='10' y='125' width='150' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='85' y='143' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Sheet A: 24 x 4 (FO1 x2)</text><rect x='175' y='125' width='38' height='100' class='ok'/><text x='194' y='170' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white;'>B: 6x16</text><text x='194' y='188' text-anchor='middle' class='sub' style='fill:white;'>(FO2 x2)</text><text x='85' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P_A = 2(24+4) = 56</text><text x='85' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P_B = 2(6+16) = 44</text><text x='200' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>56:44 = 14:11</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two rectangles showing the final folded shapes: Sheet A becomes a long thin 24 by 4 rectangle; Sheet B becomes a tall narrow 6 by 16 rectangle, with perimeters 56 and 44 giving ratio 14:11.

given.aim: compute the perimeters of sheets A and B after their respective folding operations and find their ratio

terms.meaning: both sheets are 24 cm (length) x 16 cm (width)
terms.example: a 24 x 16 rectangle is 3:2 in aspect ratio
terms.connects: the long edge is 24 cm and the short edge is 16 cm — needed to determine which dimension FO1 and FO2 each halve

terms.meaning: the fold crease lies along the 24 cm edge, so the 16 cm width is halved each time
terms.example: after 1 FO1: 24 x 8; after 2 FO1: 24 x 4
terms.connects: sheet A undergoes two FO1 folds, ending at 24 x 4

terms.meaning: the fold crease lies along the 16 cm edge, so the 24 cm length is halved each time
terms.example: after 1 FO2: 12 x 16; after 2 FO2: 6 x 16
terms.connects: sheet B undergoes two FO2 folds, ending at 6 x 16

given.plan: Apply the folding rules twice to each sheet to get final dimensions, compute each perimeter with P = 2(l + w), then find the ratio and simplify.

steps.title: Find final dimensions of sheet A after two FO1 folds
steps.formula_raw: After each FO1: width = width / 2, length unchanged
steps.apply: Start: 24 x 16. After FO1 fold 1: 24 x 8. After FO1 fold 2: 24 x 4. Final A = 24 cm x 4 cm.
steps.note: FO1 folds along the long axis, so the crease is parallel to the 24 cm edge — it is the 16 cm width that gets halved.

steps.title: Find final dimensions of sheet B after two FO2 folds
steps.formula_raw: After each FO2: length = length / 2, width unchanged
steps.apply: Start: 24 x 16. After FO2 fold 1: 12 x 16. After FO2 fold 2: 6 x 16. Final B = 6 cm x 16 cm.
steps.note: FO2 folds along the short axis, so the crease is parallel to the 16 cm edge — it is the 24 cm length that gets halved.

steps.title: Compute perimeters and their ratio
steps.note: Divide both by the GCD of 56 and 44, which is 4: 56/4 = 14, 44/4 = 11.

solution.result: The ratio of the perimeters of sheets A and B is 14 : 11.

questions.formula_note: Only the rectangle perimeter formula P = 2(l + w) is needed; the folding logic determines the final dimensions by halving the appropriate dimension twice.
